title: Lamb rogan josh
subtitle: Deep-red Kashmiri-style braised lamb with whole spices
excerpt: Lamb shoulder pieces slow-braised in a sauce of caramelised onion, tomato, and yoghurt with whole cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and a generous amount of chilli powder for the deep red colour. A long-cooked curry where the lamb collapses into the sauce. One of the great British curry-house staples.
sourceNotes: Rogan josh is a Kashmiri dish slow-cooked in yoghurt and whole spices, brought into the British-Indian restaurant repertoire in the 1970s. The British version is loosely based on Madhur Jaffrey's adaptations (Indian Cookery, 1982) and the Bangladeshi-British curry-house canon. The name means 'red' (rogan) and 'heat' (josh) — referring to the colour from chilli and Kashmiri spices.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to 3 months. Reheat on the hob from defrosted.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Better the next day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before — the flavour improves significantly overnight.

content.text: Rogan josh is a long-cook curry and needs to be treated like a braise. The lamb shoulder pieces go in early and cook for 75 to 90 minutes until the meat is completely tender and pulling apart. The sauce should reduce during cooking to a thick, glossy, dark-red gravy that clings to the lamb pieces. If the sauce looks watery at 45 minutes, remove the lid and reduce it down.

The yoghurt is added in stages and cooked in at high heat before adding water — this is the technique that prevents it splitting and gives the sauce its characteristic body. Add it in three or four tablespoons at a time and stir each addition briskly until absorbed before adding the next. This gives the sauce its distinctive rounded richness.

Heat the {{ghee}} in a wide, heavy pan over high heat. Add the {{cardamom-green}}, {{cardamom-black}}, {{cinnamon-stick}}, and {{cloves}} and fry for 30 seconds. Add the {{onion}} and cook over medium-high heat for 15 minutes until deep brown. Add the {{garlic}} and {{ginger-root}} and cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{chilli-powder}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{turmeric}} and cook for 1 minute.

Add the {{lamb-shoulder}} pieces in batches and brown on all sides. Add the {{plain-yoghurt}} 3 tablespoons at a time, stirring briskly after each addition until absorbed. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and season with salt. Add 250 ml water, bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 75 to 90 minutes until the lamb is tender. Remove the lid for the final 15 minutes to reduce the sauce. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} before serving.

items.symptom: Yoghurt has split into grainy curds
items.cause: Yoghurt added too quickly or temperature too low
items.fix: Add yoghurt tablespoon by tablespoon over high heat, stirring constantly. The heat and agitation keeps it emulsified

items.cause: Shoulder pieces too large or not enough liquid
items.fix: Cut the lamb into 3 to 4 cm pieces, no larger. Check the liquid level — add a splash of water if it's drying out

items.cause: Onion not dark enough or chilli powder quantity reduced
items.fix: The dark golden onion base gives the sauce its colour. Cook the onions until they are deep brown before adding the spices. The chilli powder is the other colour contributor

items.cause: Chilli powder quantity too high for taste
items.fix: Reduce chilli powder to 1 teaspoon for a milder version. The dish should have warmth rather than fiery heat

content.text: Rogan josh is the mid-range classic of the British curry-house menu: hotter than korma, milder than madras, and more aromatic than either. In Britain, it is most commonly made with lamb, the British-preferred meat for slow-cooked curries. The original Kashmiri dish uses dried Kashmiri chillies, which give a gentler heat and deep colour; British versions typically substitute ordinary chilli powder, which changes the character slightly. Rogan josh sits in the zone where the curry-house tradition and the home cook meet most comfortably: it rewards patience, it reheats well, and it is deeply satisfying on a cold night.
